abstract:
World models aim to learn action-controlled future prediction and have proven
essential for the development of intelligent agents. However, most existing
world models rely heavily on substantial action-labeled data and costly
training, making it challenging to adapt to novel environments with
heterogeneous actions through limited interactions. This limitation can hinder
their applicability across broader domains. To overcome this limitation, we
propose AdaWorld, an innovative world model learning approach that enables
efficient adaptation. The key idea is to incorporate action information during
the pretraining of world models. This is achieved by extracting latent actions
from videos in a self-supervised manner, capturing the most critical
transitions between frames. We then develop an autoregressive world model that
conditions on these latent actions. This learning paradigm enables highly
adaptable world models, facilitating efficient transfer and learning of new
actions even with limited interactions and finetuning. Our comprehensive
experiments across multiple environments demonstrate that AdaWorld achieves
superior performance in both simulation quality and visual planning.

keyInsights: Human-like adaptability in new environments may stem from transferrable action representations learned from observations.
Disentangling action information from complex contextual details in "in-the-wild" videos is crucial for learning context-invariant action representations.
Incorporating action-awareness during large-scale pretraining, even without explicit action labels, leads to more adaptable and data-efficient world models.
originalProblem: World models typically require large amounts of action-labeled data for training and costly retraining for adaptation.
Adapting existing world models to new environments with heterogeneous actions or limited interactions is computationally intensive.
Current approaches are either action-agnostic (lack control, require extensive fine-tuning) or action-specific (poor generalization).
results: Achieves superior action transfer to unseen contexts (e.g., FVD of 767.0 vs. 1545.2 for action-agnostic baseline on LIBERO) and higher human success rates (70.5% vs. 20%).
Demonstrates more efficient world model adaptation, attaining higher PSNR and lower LPIPS with limited finetuning steps across diverse environments (Habitat, Minecraft, DMLab, nuScenes).
Improves visual planning success rates in game environments (Procgen) and robot manipulation tasks (VP2 benchmark), often outperforming finetuned baselines without finetuning.
solution: A Latent Action Autoencoder (LAE) extracts compact, context-invariant "latent actions" from unlabeled videos in a self-supervised manner.
An autoregressive world model, initialized from Stable Video Diffusion, is pretrained to predict future frames conditioned on these self-supervised latent actions.
The LAE's information bottleneck design, optimized with a beta-VAE loss, forces the model to capture only critical action-related variations.
paper_summary.summary: AdaWorld introduces a novel approach to world model learning that extracts context-invariant latent actions from unlabeled videos, enabling efficient adaptation to new environments. This method achieves superior action transfer (e.g., 70.5% human success rate on LIBERO vs. 20% for baseline) and faster world model adaptation with limited data, improving visual planning success rates in game and robotic tasks.

title: AdaWorld: Learning Adaptable World Models with Latent Actions

abstract: We present DINO-world, a powerful generalist video world model trained to predict future frames in the latent space of DINOv2. By leveraging a pre-trained image encoder and training a future predictor on a large-scale uncurated video dataset, DINO-world learns the temporal dynamics of diverse scenes, from driving and indoor scenes to simulated environments. We show that DINO-world outperforms previous models on a variety of video prediction benchmarks, e.g. segmentation and depth forecasting, and demonstrates strong understanding of intuitive physics. Furthermore, we show that it is possible to fine-tune the predictor on observation-action trajectories. The resulting action-conditioned world model can be used for planning by simulating candidate trajectories in latent space.

keyInsights: Leveraging strong, frozen self-supervised image features (DINOv2) is effective for building efficient latent-space video world models, significantly reducing computational overhead.
Learning general temporal dynamics from massive uncurated video datasets is crucial for broad applicability and reduces reliance on expensive action-annotated data.
Operating in a semantic latent space (DINOv2 features) improves computational efficiency and prediction quality compared to pixel-based approaches, allowing the model to focus on meaningful dynamics.
originalProblem: Traditional pixel-space generative world models are computationally expensive and require vast resources for training.
Many existing world models are specialized for narrow domains and struggle to generalize due to limited or domain-specific training data.
High-quality, large-scale video datasets annotated with actions are scarce and costly, limiting progress in action-conditioned world modeling.
results: DINO-world consistently outperforms pixel-based (COSMOS) and jointly-trained (V-JEPA) models in dense feature forecasting, achieving 47.0% mIoU on VSPW segmentation (vs. 40.7% for COSMOS) and lower RMSE on KITTI depth.
The model demonstrates strong intuitive physics understanding, achieving 91.3% accuracy on IntPhys, comparable to or better than other large-scale models.
Pre-training substantially improves action-conditioned planning, with fine-tuned DINO-world achieving a 93.8% success rate on the Wall environment compared to 87.1% for training from scratch.
solution: A two-stage approach: pre-training an unconditional video world model in a latent space and then fine-tuning it with action conditioning for specific control tasks.
Utilizes a frozen, pre-trained DINOv2 ViT-B/14 as the frame encoder to map input video frames to a high-level, semantic latent space of patch tokens.

Employs a lightweight cross-attention predictor architecture trained with a smooth L1 loss on next-frame prediction, incorporating Rotary Position Embeddings (RoPE) for spatiotemporal reasoning and variable frame rates.
paper_summary.summary: Meta FAIR's DINO-world introduces an efficient generalist video world model by leveraging a frozen DINOv2 encoder to predict future states in a semantic latent space, outperforming pixel-based models in dense feature forecasting and enabling effective action-conditioned planning from uncurated video data.

title: Back to the Features: DINO as a Foundation for Video World Models

abstract:
This paper presents a novel approach that enables autoregressive video
generation with high efficiency. We propose to reformulate the video generation
problem as a non-quantized autoregressive modeling of temporal frame-by-frame
prediction and spatial set-by-set prediction. Unlike raster-scan prediction in
prior autoregressive models or joint distribution modeling of fixed-length
tokens in diffusion models, our approach maintains the causal property of
GPT-style models for flexible in-context capabilities, while leveraging
bidirectional modeling within individual frames for efficiency. With the
proposed approach, we train a novel video autoregressive model without vector
quantization, termed NOVA. Our results demonstrate that NOVA surpasses prior
autoregressive video models in data efficiency, inference speed, visual
fidelity, and video fluency, even with a much smaller model capacity, i.e.,
0.6B parameters. NOVA also outperforms state-of-the-art image diffusion models
in text-to-image generation tasks, with a significantly lower training cost.
Additionally, NOVA generalizes well across extended video durations and enables
diverse zero-shot applications in one unified model. Code and models are
publicly available at this https URL

keyInsights: Successfully combining non-quantized continuous latent representations with an autoregressive framework enables high-fidelity video generation while retaining AR models' flexibility and in-context learning capabilities.
A hierarchical autoregressive structure, with distinct temporal and spatial prediction mechanisms, allows for improved scalability, coherence, and variable-length video generation and extrapolation.

Architectural design choices, such as the Scaling and Shift Layer for robust motion and post-normalization for training stability, are critical for developing efficient and high-performing large-scale generative models in the complex video domain.
originalProblem: Traditional autoregressive (AR) vision models using vector quantization (VQ-VAE) face a fidelity-compression trade-off, leading to escalating computational costs for high-resolution or long videos.

Diffusion models, despite high fidelity, often lack the flexibility for variable-length video generation and intrinsic in-context learning, typically requiring fixed frame counts or additional inference steps.
Existing AR video models struggle with scalability and high computational demands, making efficient large-scale, high-quality video synthesis challenging.
results: NOVA achieved a GenEval score of 0.75 for text-to-image generation, setting a new benchmark for AR models, with a significantly low training cost of 127 GPU days for a 0.6B parameter model.

For text-to-video generation, a 0.6B parameter NOVA model attained a VBench score of 80.12, performing competitively with state-of-the-art diffusion models and outperforming larger AR models like Emu3 (8B parameters) in various metrics.

The model demonstrated robust zero-shot generalization for video extrapolation (up to 3x training length) and seamless multitasking for text-to-image, image-to-video, and text-to-video generation within a unified framework, achieving a processing speed of 2.75 FPS for video generation on a single NVIDIA A100-40G GPU.
solution: NOVA implements a non-quantized autoregressive approach by utilizing a frozen 3D Variational Autoencoder (VAE) to encode video frames into a compact, continuous latent space, bypassing discrete tokenization.

It employs a hierarchical autoregressive framework that separates temporal frame-by-frame prediction from spatial set-by-set prediction, enabling causal generation, efficient decoding with KV-cache, and flexible video lengths.

The model integrates a Scaling and Shift Layer to maintain motion coherence across frames and incorporates post-normalization layers within transformers to enhance architectural stability and mitigate optimization challenges during large-scale training.
paper_summary.summary: NOVA, from BAAI and collaborators, introduces the first non-quantized autoregressive model for video generation, achieving state-of-the-art text-to-image alignment with a GenEval score of 0.75 at reduced training costs (127 GPU days for 0.6B parameters). This model also rivals leading diffusion and autoregressive video models in performance, reaching a VBench score of 80.12, while offering enhanced flexibility for variable-length and zero-shot multi-task video generation.

title: Autoregressive Video Generation without Vector Quantization

abstract: Recent advances in diffusion transformers have empowered video generation models to generate high-quality video clips from texts or images. However, world models with the ability to predict long-horizon futures from past observations and actions remain underexplored, especially for general-purpose scenarios and various forms of actions. To bridge this gap, we introduce Astra, an interactive general world model that generates real-world futures for diverse scenarios (e.g., autonomous driving, robot grasping) with precise action interactions (e.g., camera motion, robot action). We propose an autoregressive denoising architecture and use temporal causal attention to aggregate past observations and support streaming outputs. We use a noise-augmented history memory to avoid over-reliance on past frames to balance responsiveness with temporal coherence. For precise action control, we introduce an action-aware adapter that directly injects action signals into the denoising process. We further develop a mixture of action experts that dynamically route heterogeneous action modalities, enhancing versatility across diverse real-world tasks such as exploration, manipulation, and camera control. Astra achieves interactive, consistent, and general long-term video prediction and supports various forms of interactions. Experiments across multiple datasets demonstrate the improvements of Astra in fidelity, long-range prediction, and action alignment over existing state-of-the-art world models.

keyInsights: Leveraging pre-trained video diffusion backbones with lightweight, fine-tuned adapters (ACT-Adapter) allows for efficient integration of action control without retraining the entire generative model.

Injecting random noise into historical conditioning frames (Noise-Augmented History Memory) effectively mitigates 'visual inertia', compelling the model to actively respond to new actions rather than merely copying past visual context.

Combining Action-Free Guidance (AFG) at inference with an autoregressive denoising paradigm strikes a balance between maintaining long-term temporal coherence and ensuring sharp, precise responsiveness to action inputs.
originalProblem: Existing Text-to-Video (T2V) or Image-to-Video (I2V) diffusion models are passive, unable to adapt dynamically to real-time stimuli or agent actions for long, coherent trajectories.
Prior autoregressive world models suffer from error accumulation and temporal drift, struggling to balance long-term consistency with instantaneous responsiveness to new action inputs.
Unifying heterogeneous action modalities (e.g., camera movements, robot control) within a single, versatile framework poses a significant challenge for interactive world models.
results: Astra significantly outperforms state-of-the-art video generation and world modeling approaches on the Astra-Bench benchmark, achieving lower instruction following errors (e.g., 1.23 Rotation Error vs. 2.20 for YUME) and higher VBench scores for visual quality and consistency.

Ablation studies confirm the effectiveness of core components: ACT-Adapter for action integration (0.669 instruction following), Action-Free Guidance for enhanced responsiveness (improving from 0.545 to 0.669), Noise-as-Mask for mitigating visual inertia (improving from 0.359 to 0.669), and MoAE for versatility.

The model demonstrates strong out-of-domain generalization, generating coherent, action-conditioned rollouts in unseen environments like indoor scenes and Minecraft, while maintaining high parameter efficiency (366.8M trainable parameters).
solution: An autoregressive denoising framework, using flow matching, iteratively predicts future video chunks by augmenting a pre-trained video Diffusion Transformer (Wan-2.1).

An Action-Aware Flow Transformer (ACT-Adapter) injects action features directly into every transformer block of the denoising model, enabling precise and instantaneous action control while preserving pre-trained knowledge.

A Mixture of Action Experts (MoAE) processes diverse action modalities by mapping them to a shared space and dynamically aggregating outputs from modality-specific MLPs, enhancing versatility and scalability.
paper_summary.summary: ASTRA, developed by researchers from Tsinghua University and Kuaishou Technology, introduces an interactive general world model capable of generating real-world futures with precise action interactions and long-term consistency. The model leverages an autoregressive denoising framework to enhance a pre-trained video diffusion backbone, demonstrating superior performance in instruction following and visual quality across diverse interactive scenarios.

title: Astra: General Interactive World Model with Autoregressive Denoising

abstract: World models, which predict future transitions from past observation and action sequences, have shown great promise for improving data efficiency in sequential decision-making. However, existing world models often require extensive domain-specific training and still produce low-fidelity, coarse predictions, limiting their usefulness in complex environments. In contrast, video diffusion models trained on large-scale internet data have demonstrated impressive capabilities in generating high-quality videos that capture diverse real-world dynamics. In this work, we present Vid2World, a general approach for leveraging and transferring pre-trained video diffusion models into interactive world models. To bridge the gap, Vid2World systematically explores video diffusion causalization, reshaping both the architecture and training objective of pre-trained models to enable autoregressive generation. Additionally, it incorporates a causal action guidance mechanism to enhance action controllability in the resulting interactive world models. Extensive experiments across multiple domains, including robot manipulation, 3D game simulation, and open-world navigation, demonstrate that our method offers a scalable and effective pathway for repurposing highly capable video diffusion models into interactive world models.
title: Vid2World: Crafting Video Diffusion Models to Interactive World Models

abstract:
Efficient video tokenization remains a key bottleneck in learning general
purpose vision models that are capable of processing long video sequences.
Prevailing approaches are restricted to encoding videos to a fixed number of
tokens, where too few tokens will result in overly lossy encodings, and too
many tokens will result in prohibitively long sequence lengths. In this work,
we introduce ElasticTok, a method that conditions on prior frames to adaptively
encode a frame into a variable number of tokens. To enable this in a
computationally scalable way, we propose a masking technique that drops a
random number of tokens at the end of each frames's token encoding. During
inference, ElasticTok can dynamically allocate tokens when needed -- more
complex data can leverage more tokens, while simpler data only needs a few
tokens. Our empirical evaluations on images and video demonstrate the
effectiveness of our approach in efficient token usage, paving the way for
future development of more powerful multimodal models, world models, and
agents.

keyInsights: Visual token allocation can be dynamically adjusted based on the inherent informational complexity of the content, leading to significant efficiency gains without compromising reconstruction quality.
By training an autoencoder with random masking of latent tokens, a single model can learn to effectively encode visual inputs into variable-length representations.
The system offers flexible inference modes, allowing users to dynamically allocate tokens to meet a target encoding length or a desired reconstruction quality threshold.
The method is modality-agnostic and scalable, effectively integrating with long-context transformers to process extremely long video sequences efficiently.
originalProblem: Fixed-length visual tokenization for long videos leads to a trade-off between overly lossy encodings and prohibitive computational costs due to excessively long sequence lengths.
Current methods require allocating a large, fixed number of tokens to account for worst-case complex inputs, resulting in wasted computational resources on simpler visual data.
Efficient video tokenization remains a key bottleneck for developing general-purpose vision models and intelligent agents capable of processing and understanding long temporal sequences.
results: ElasticTok achieves reconstruction quality comparable to fixed-token baselines while using 2x to 5x fewer tokens on average for both images and videos, depending on the target fidelity.
The system dynamically allocates more tokens to visually complex areas (e.g., high-frequency details, motion, scene changes) and fewer to simpler, static content.

Downstream vision-language models finetuned with ElasticTok's adaptive tokens match the accuracy of models trained with fixed-token baselines, demonstrating efficiency with little to no loss in task performance.

Reconstruction quality improves progressively with more tokens, enabling flexible accuracy-compute trade-offs, and shows a strong correlation (0.77 for images) between token allocation and high-frequency content.
solution: ElasticTok modifies standard autoencoder training by sampling a variable number of tokens to keep (ℓ) from a maximum capacity (N) for each input and conditioning the encoder on a corresponding binary mask.
The encoder's latent representation is masked element-wise to drop unselected tokens, and the decoder reconstructs the input from this variable-length representation.

For long videos, the approach processes content in blocks with independent masking and leverages Blockwise RingAttention to efficiently scale to very long sequences while maintaining contextual awareness without adding parameters for longer contexts.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers at UC Berkeley and Google DeepMind developed ElasticTok, an adaptive visual tokenizer for images and videos that dynamically adjusts token allocation based on content complexity. This method achieves comparable reconstruction quality using 2 to 5 times fewer tokens than fixed-token baselines, enabling efficient processing of long video sequences for large models.

title: ElasticTok: Adaptive Tokenization for Image and Video

abstract: World models that forecast scene evolution by generating future video frames devote the bulk of their capacity to photometric details, yet the resulting predictions often remain geometrically inconsistent. We present VGGT-World, a geometry world model that side-steps video generation entirely and instead forecasts the temporal evolution of frozen geometry-foundation-model (GFM) features. Concretely, we repurpose the latent tokens of a frozen VGGT as the world state and train a lightweight temporal flow transformer to autoregressively predict their future trajectory. Two technical challenges arise in this high-dimensional (d=1024) feature space: (i) standard velocity-prediction flow matching collapses, and (ii) autoregressive rollout suffers from compounding exposure bias. We address the first with a clean-target (z-prediction) parameterization that yields a substantially higher signal-to-noise ratio, and the second with a two-stage latent flow-forcing curriculum that progressively conditions the model on its own partially denoised rollouts. Experiments on KITTI, Cityscapes, and TartanAir demonstrate that VGGT-World significantly outperforms the strongest baselines in depth forecasting while running 3.6-5 times faster with only 0.43B trainable parameters, establishing frozen GFM features as an effective and efficient predictive state for 3D world modeling.

keyInsights: Directly forecasting GFM latent features is more effective for geometrically consistent 3D world modeling than relying on video generation which often compromises structural integrity for visual plausibility.
Standard velocity-prediction flow matching is ineffective in high-dimensional, weakly compressed geometry latent spaces; z-prediction flow offers a more stable and effective alternative.
Forecasting the unseen future can paradoxically improve the geometric estimation of the present, as aggregating predicted future observations refines the current moment's geometric belief.
originalProblem: Existing video-centric world models prioritize photometric realism, often leading to geometrically inconsistent future predictions like distorted 3D structures or mislocalized objects.
These video generation-based models are computationally intensive, requiring significant GPU resources and extended training times for both training and inference.

Geometry Foundation Models (GFMs) excel at inferring 3D geometry from observed views but lack the ability to predict how this geometry will evolve over time, which is crucial for embodied AI planning.
results: Achieved up to 32% relative improvement in AbsRel for depth forecasting on Cityscapes and 21% on KITTI compared to prior state-of-the-art baselines.
Demonstrated superior 3D point cloud forecasting accuracy on TartanAir, outperforming pixel-space generative models on metrics like Accuracy, Completeness, and Chamfer Distance.

Reduced inference latency by 3.6x to 5x (1.9 seconds per frame vs. 6.9-9.5 seconds) with a significantly smaller trainable parameter count (0.43 billion vs. 2.7-12 billion) compared to leading video-generation models.
solution: The framework uses a frozen, pretrained VGGT encoder to extract high-dimensional (1024-D) geometry states from observed frames, decoupling spatial representation from temporal dynamics.

A lightweight temporal flow transformer is employed to autoregressively predict future geometry states in temporal chunks, using a z-prediction parameterization for flow matching to handle high-dimensional latent space challenges.

A two-stage latent flow forcing curriculum, involving teaching-forcing and trajectory-consistent flow forcing, is introduced to mitigate exposure bias in autoregressive rollouts and enhance robustness.
paper_summary.summary: VGGT-World presents a geometry world modeling framework that directly forecasts the temporal evolution of 3D scene geometry using latent features from a frozen Geometry Foundation Model (GFM). This approach achieves superior geometric consistency in future depth and point cloud predictions, improving metrics like AbsRel by up to 32%, while significantly reducing computational overhead with 3.6x to 5x faster inference and a much smaller trainable footprint compared to video-centric world models.

title: VGGT-World: Transforming VGGT into an Autoregressive Geometry World Model

abstract: Autoregressive (AR) video generative models rely on video tokenizers that compress pixels into discrete token sequences. The length of these token sequences is crucial for balancing reconstruction quality against downstream generation computational cost. Traditional video tokenizers apply a uniform token assignment across temporal blocks of different videos, often wasting tokens on simple, static, or repetitive segments while underserving dynamic or complex ones. To address this inefficiency, we introduce $\textbf{EVATok}$, a framework to produce $\textbf{E}$fficient $\textbf{V}$ideo $\textbf{A}$daptive $\textbf{Tok}$enizers. Our framework estimates optimal token assignments for each video to achieve the best quality-cost trade-off, develops lightweight routers for fast prediction of these optimal assignments, and trains adaptive tokenizers that encode videos based on the assignments predicted by routers. We demonstrate that EVATok delivers substantial improvements in efficiency and overall quality for video reconstruction and downstream AR generation. Enhanced by our advanced training recipe that integrates video semantic encoders, EVATok achieves superior reconstruction and state-of-the-art class-to-video generation on UCF-101, with at least 24.4% savings in average token usage compared to the prior state-of-the-art LARP and our fixed-length baseline.

keyInsights: Content-adaptive token allocation, driven by a principled "proxy reward" metric, allows for simultaneous improvements in efficiency and reconstruction/generation quality.
A lightweight router can effectively predict optimal token assignments on the fly, making adaptive tokenization practical for large-scale video processing without brute-force search.
Training the final tokenizer with router-predicted optimal assignments helps bridge the training-inference gap, leading to improved performance over using a proxy model alone.
originalProblem: Traditional video tokenizers allocate a fixed number of tokens, inefficiently handling the varying information density within video content.
Existing adaptive tokenization methods often rely on suboptimal heuristics or batch-dependent strategies, failing to identify an optimal quality-cost trade-off for individual video samples.
Fixed token allocation leads to wasted computational resources for simple video segments and insufficient detail for complex ones, compromising fidelity.
results: Achieved 56% token savings on WebVid and 42% on UCF-101 for comparable or improved reconstruction quality (rFVD) compared to fixed uniform assignments.
The router-guided final tokenizer reduced token length by 29.6% for reconstruction on WebVid and 24.4% on UCF/K600 with comparable or better quality.

Downstream AR generation models using EVATok tokens improved generation quality (gFVD) on UCF-101 while using 27.7% fewer tokens, establishing a new state-of-the-art gFVD of 48 with 26.2% fewer tokens than previous methods.
solution: Introduced a "proxy reward" metric to quantify the quality-cost trade-off for any token assignment.
Trained a Q-Former style proxy tokenizer to assess reconstruction quality, then brute-force searched for optimal assignments for a large dataset of videos.
Developed a lightweight, ViT-like router to efficiently predict these content-adaptive optimal assignments in a single pass, and trained a final adaptive video tokenizer using these predictions.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers at The University of Hong Kong and ByteDance Seed developed EVATok, an adaptive tokenization framework that dynamically assigns tokens based on video content complexity. This method enables more efficient video reconstruction and generation, reducing token usage by over 24% for reconstruction and 26% for generation on UCF-101, while achieving superior quality and new state-of-the-art performance in downstream autoregressive tasks.

title: EVATok: Adaptive Length Video Tokenization for Efficient Visual Autoregressive Generation

abstract: World models provide a powerful framework for simulating environment dynamics conditioned on actions or instructions, enabling downstream tasks such as action planning or policy learning. Recent approaches leverage world models as learned simulators, but its application to decision-time planning remains computationally prohibitive for real-time control. A key bottleneck lies in latent representations: conventional tokenizers encode each observation into hundreds of tokens, making planning both slow and resource-intensive. To address this, we propose CompACT, a discrete tokenizer that compresses each observation into as few as 8 tokens, drastically reducing computational cost while preserving essential information for planning. An action-conditioned world model that occupies CompACT tokenizer achieves competitive planning performance with orders-of-magnitude faster planning, offering a practical step toward real-world deployment of world models.

keyInsights: Effective planning in world models prioritizes compact, semantic representations of decision-critical information over photorealistic perceptual details.

Leveraging frozen foundation models (like DINOv3) during tokenization encourages the emergence of modular, semantically meaningful latent tokens that are highly beneficial for capturing action-relevant state changes.
Decoupling the generation of planning-critical semantic content from the synthesis of high-frequency perceptual details allows for extreme compression without sacrificing planning performance.
originalProblem: World models, while powerful, face a computational bottleneck due to high-fidelity visual representations (hundreds to thousands of latent tokens per image).
Attention-based architectures, common in generative world models, scale quadratically with token count, making real-time planning prohibitively slow (e.g., minutes per episode).
The focus on pixel-perfect image reconstruction in existing tokenizers is misaligned with the actual requirements for efficient planning, which needs compact, action-relevant representations.
results: Achieved competitive planning accuracy on visual navigation tasks (ATE 1.330, RPE 0.390) while delivering approximately a 40x speedup in planning latency (5.78s vs. 178.78s) compared to SD-VAE baselines.
Demonstrated that using latent-space cost functions during planning provides an ~80x overall speedup with minimal accuracy degradation.

CompACT (16 tokens) showed a 3x lower Action Prediction Error (APE) and was 5.2x faster in action-conditioned video prediction compared to a 256-token baseline, indicating better preservation of action-driven dynamics.
solution: Developed CompACT, a compact discrete tokenizer that encodes images into 8-16 discrete tokens by leveraging a frozen, pretrained vision encoder (DINOv3) and a latent resampler with Finite Scalar Quantization (FSQ).

Introduced a generative decoding strategy for CompACT, which, instead of direct pixel reconstruction, learns to generate higher-fidelity latent tokens from a separate pretrained target tokenizer using masked generative modeling.
Trained action-conditioned world models directly on CompACT's compact discrete latent space using masked generative modeling to predict future states for efficient Model Predictive Control (MPC).
paper_summary.summary: A compact discrete tokenizer, CompACT, allows world models to achieve up to an 80x speedup in planning latency for navigation and robotic manipulation while maintaining competitive performance. This is achieved by encoding observations into as few as 8-16 tokens, prioritizing planning-critical semantic information over photorealistic detail.

title: Planning in 8 Tokens: A Compact Discrete Tokenizer for Latent World Model

abstract:
World models have attracted increasing attention in autonomous driving for
their ability to forecast potential future scenarios. In this paper, we propose
BEVWorld, a novel framework that transforms multimodal sensor inputs into a
unified and compact Bird's Eye View (BEV) latent space for holistic environment
modeling. The proposed world model consists of two main components: a
multi-modal tokenizer and a latent BEV sequence diffusion model. The
multi-modal tokenizer first encodes heterogeneous sensory data, and its decoder
reconstructs the latent BEV tokens into LiDAR and surround-view image
observations via ray-casting rendering in a self-supervised manner. This
enables joint modeling and bidirectional encoding-decoding of panoramic imagery
and point cloud data within a shared spatial representation. On top of this,
the latent BEV sequence diffusion model performs temporally consistent
forecasting of future scenes, conditioned on high-level action tokens, enabling
scene-level reasoning over time. Extensive experiments demonstrate the
effectiveness of BEVWorld on autonomous driving benchmarks, showcasing its
capability in realistic future scene generation and its benefits for downstream
tasks such as perception and motion prediction.

keyInsights: Unified multi-modal fusion in a BEV latent space, combining both camera and LiDAR inputs, is crucial for superior environmental representation and reconstruction quality compared to single-modality approaches.
Explicit ray-based rendering, leveraging sensor pose and imaging geometry, is essential for accurately reconstructing multi-view images and LiDAR point clouds from BEV features.

Decoupling the modeling of low-level sensor details from high-level behavioral dynamics within a diffusion framework significantly enhances the realism, temporal consistency, and controllability of future scene generation.
originalProblem: Existing Driving World Models (DWMs) struggled with realistic 3D spatial modeling, often being limited to 2D video generation or single-modality 3D predictions.
Integrating and fusing heterogeneous multi-sensor data (e.g., cameras and LiDAR) into a unified and compact representation for holistic environment understanding remained a significant challenge.
Many DWMs adopted end-to-end architectures that failed to explicitly decouple low-level pixel/voxel details from high-level behavioral dynamics, compromising generation quality and realism.
results: The multi-modal tokenizer, when used for pre-training, improved 3D object detection performance on nuScenes by 8.4% in NDS (from 0.392 to 0.476) and 13.4% in mAP (from 0.253 to 0.387).
The latent BEV sequence diffusion model achieved a Chamfer Distance of 0.73 for 3-second LiDAR predictions on nuScenes, outperforming prior SOTA methods like 4D-Occ (1.40).

BEVWorld generated high-fidelity, action-conditioned multi-view images and LiDAR point clouds, achieving an FID of 19.0 and FVD of 154.0 on nuScenes without manual labeling conditions, demonstrating robust controllability and realism.
solution: BEVWorld introduces a multi-modal tokenizer that unifies diverse camera images and LiDAR point clouds into a compact, low-dimensional Bird’s Eye View (BEV) latent representation, enabling high-fidelity reconstruction via a NeRF-based ray rendering network.
A latent BEV sequence diffusion model forecasts multiple future BEV frames simultaneously, conditioned on ego-vehicle action tokens, to avoid cumulative errors common in auto-regressive approaches.

The framework explicitly decouples low-level sensor data compression and reconstruction (tokenizer) from high-level behavioral dynamics prediction (diffusion model) to improve overall scene generation quality.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers at Baidu Inc. developed BEVWorld, a multimodal world model for autonomous driving that forecasts future scenes by converting heterogeneous sensor data into a unified Bird’s Eye View (BEV) latent space. The model achieves superior LiDAR prediction quality with a Chamfer Distance of 0.73 for 3-second predictions on nuScenes and significantly boosts downstream 3D object detection performance by 8.4% NDS through its pre-training capabilities.

title: BEVWorld: A Multimodal World Simulator for Autonomous Driving via  Scene-Level BEV Latents

abstract: Recent successes in autoregressive (AR) generation models, such as the GPT series in natural language processing, have motivated efforts to replicate this success in visual tasks. Some works attempt to extend this approach to autonomous driving by building video-based world models capable of generating realistic future video sequences and predicting ego states. However, prior works tend to produce unsatisfactory results, as the classic GPT framework is designed to handle 1D contextual information, such as text, and lacks the inherent ability to model the spatial and temporal dynamics essential for video generation. In this paper, we present DrivingWorld, a GPT-style world model for autonomous driving, featuring several spatial-temporal fusion mechanisms. This design enables effective modeling of both spatial and temporal dynamics, facilitating high-fidelity, long-duration video generation. Specifically, we propose a next-state prediction strategy to model temporal coherence between consecutive frames and apply a next-token prediction strategy to capture spatial information within each frame. To further enhance generalization ability, we propose a novel masking strategy and reweighting strategy for token prediction to mitigate long-term drifting issues and enable precise control. Our work demonstrates the ability to produce high-fidelity and consistent video clips of over 40 seconds in duration, which is over 2 times longer than state-of-the-art driving world models. Experiments show that, in contrast to prior works, our method achieves superior visual quality and significantly more accurate controllable future video generation. Our code is available at this https URL.

keyInsights: Decoupling spatio-temporal processing into distinct temporal and multimodal attention layers within a GPT-style architecture significantly enhances efficiency and improves the quality of long-duration video generation for complex driving scenes.

Strategically corrupting training data through a random masking strategy effectively addresses the content drifting problem inherent in long-term autoregressive generation, leading to more robust and temporally consistent outputs.

Prioritizing ego-state information through balanced attention is crucial for maintaining precise control over generated trajectories in visually rich environments, enabling practical application in autonomous driving planning.
A temporal-aware VQVAE tokenizer, which explicitly models temporal dependencies, is essential for generating high-fidelity and temporally coherent video sequences compared to single-image VQGANs.
originalProblem: Vanilla GPT frameworks, designed for 1D text sequences, struggle to effectively model the complex spatial and temporal dynamics required for high-fidelity video generation in autonomous driving.
Existing driving world models often face limitations in the duration, visual fidelity, or precise controllability of generated predictions, typically producing much shorter video clips.
Autoregressive generation for long video sequences is prone to content drifting and error accumulation, leading to degradation over time and reduced robustness.
Autonomous driving systems require robust prediction for out-of-distribution and long-tail scenarios, which traditional data-driven models find challenging to cover comprehensively.
results: Successfully generates high-fidelity and temporally consistent driving videos exceeding 40 seconds (400 frames at 10 Hz) from just 15 conditioning frames, a substantial improvement over prior state-of-the-art models.

Achieves comparable state-of-the-art performance on the NuScenes dataset (FID: 7.4; FVD: 90.9) in zero-shot settings, demonstrating robust generalization and superior generation quality over much longer durations.

Ablation studies confirm the Random Masking Strategy (RMS) substantially mitigates content drifting, increasing FVD scores by 4% to 32% without it, and the architecture is significantly more memory-efficient than vanilla GPT-2, avoiding OOM errors for long sequences.
solution: A hybrid token prediction strategy with a Temporal-multimodal Fusion Module decouples spatio-temporal processing, using causal attention for temporal layers and bidirectional attention for multimodal layers within a frame.

A Temporal-aware Vector Quantized Tokenizer embeds self-attention layers before and after VQGAN quantization to capture temporal dependencies, alongside a relative vehicle pose tokenizer for continuous control.
Introduced a Random Masking Strategy (RMS) during training to randomly replace ground-truth tokens, mitigating content drifting and enhancing robustness during long-term autoregressive inference.
Utilized Balanced Attention by increasing attention weights for pose tokens, ensuring precise control over ego-vehicle trajectory despite the imbalance between image and pose tokens.
paper_summary.summary: DrivingWorld introduces a GPT-style world model for autonomous driving, employing a hybrid token prediction strategy and temporal-aware tokenizers to generate high-fidelity, controllable driving videos exceeding 40 seconds. The model achieves comparable state-of-the-art performance on public benchmarks while significantly extending generation duration and enhancing spatio-temporal consistency.

title: DrivingWorld: Constructing World Model for Autonomous Driving via Video GPT

abstract:
A world model is essential for an agent to predict the future and plan in
domains such as autonomous driving and robotics. To achieve this, recent
advancements have focused on video generation, which has gained significant
attention due to the impressive success of diffusion models. However, these
models require substantial computational resources. To address these
challenges, we propose a world model leveraging object-centric representation
space using slot attention, guided by language instructions. Our model
perceives the current state as an object-centric representation and predicts
future states in this representation space conditioned on natural language
instructions. This approach results in a more compact and computationally
efficient model compared to diffusion-based generative alternatives.
Furthermore, it flexibly predicts future states based on language instructions,
and offers a significant advantage in manipulation tasks where object
recognition is crucial. In this paper, we demonstrate that our latent
predictive world model surpasses generative world models in visuo-linguo-motor
control tasks, achieving superior sample and computation efficiency. We also
investigate the generalization performance of the proposed method and explore
various strategies for predicting actions using object-centric representations.

keyInsights: Language instructions are most effectively integrated by conditioning the world model's future state predictions (LSlotFormer) rather than directly feeding them to the action decoder.
Access to future state predictions from the world model is indispensable for effective action control, with optimal performance achieved using a specific lookahead horizon.
Transformer encoders processing slots grouped by timestep are more effective for action decoding from object-centric representations compared to simpler MLP layers or slot-wise grouping.
originalProblem: State-of-the-art diffusion-based world models for language-guided manipulation require extensive labeled datasets and incur substantial computational costs for training and inference.
Many existing world models lack explicit, interpretable representations of individual objects, which is critical for precise interaction in robotic manipulation tasks.
There is a challenge in enabling flexible language-guided control for robotic agents in a computationally feasible manner.
results: The proposed method achieved a 50.0% task success rate (at 0.05 unit distance threshold), significantly outperforming diffusion-based baselines like Susie+SAVi (13.0%) and Seer variants (0.0-0.5%).
The model demonstrated superior computational efficiency, being up to 85% faster in training and 74% faster in inference compared to state-of-the-art diffusion-based world models.
It showed promising generalization to unseen block configurations (38.0% success rate) and novel task configurations, indicating a more robust understanding of manipulation concepts than baselines.
solution: A Language-Guided Slot World Model (LSlotFormer) was developed, which uses a pre-trained SAVi encoder to extract object-centric 'slots' and a T5-base encoder for language instructions.
LSlotFormer, a modified transformer-based architecture, predicts future slot trajectories conditioned on language instructions, trained to reconstruct ground truth future slot representations.

A predictive control action decoder was implemented to translate current and predicted future slot trajectories into executable robotic actions, with its architecture optimized through ablation studies.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers at Seoul National University developed a language-guided object-centric world model that effectively predicts future environmental states for robotic manipulation. This model utilizes structured object representations and natural language instructions to achieve higher task success rates and improved computational efficiency, significantly outperforming diffusion-based generative world models in terms of both performance and speed.

title: Object-Centric World Model for Language-Guided Manipulation

abstract: Forecasting what will happen next is a critical skill for general-purpose systems that plan or act in the world at different levels of abstraction. In this paper, we identify a strong correlation between a vision model's perceptual ability and its generalist forecasting performance over short time horizons. This trend holds across a diverse set of pretrained models-including those trained generatively-and across multiple levels of abstraction, from raw pixels to depth, point tracks, and object motion. The result is made possible by a novel generalist forecasting framework that operates on any frozen vision backbone: we train latent diffusion models to forecast future features in the frozen representation space, which are then decoded via lightweight, task-specific readouts. To enable consistent evaluation across tasks, we introduce distributional metrics that compare distributional properties directly in the space of downstream tasks and apply this framework to nine models and four tasks. Our results highlight the value of bridging representation learning and generative modeling for temporally grounded video understanding.

keyInsights: A strong positive correlation exists between a vision model's perceptual ability on observed frames and its generalist forecasting performance over short time horizons, particularly for models with temporal video supervision.

Temporal supervision during pre-training is crucial for generalizable video representations capable of forecasting; models pre-trained on static images or with only language supervision perform poorly.

Distributional metrics like Fréchet Distance and variance are essential for evaluating forecasting models as they capture the stochasticity and diversity of future predictions, revealing aspects not evident from mean-based metrics.
originalProblem: Generalist computer vision models primarily focus on perception (understanding the present), lacking the ability to anticipate future states of the world under uncertainty.
Existing video forecasting methods are often task-specific, deterministic, or struggle with modeling multi-modal futures and continuous temporal trajectories across various levels of abstraction.
A consistent framework and robust evaluation metrics were needed to compare different vision backbones' forecasting capabilities fairly and to account for the inherent stochasticity of future events.
results: Models with stronger perceptual performance, especially those with temporal video pre-training (e.g., 4DS-e), consistently achieve better generalist forecasting performance.

Video synthesis models like W.A.L.T. excel at low-level tasks such as pixel and depth prediction (e.g., FD of 8.60 for pixels), outperforming masked video models, but show mixed performance on higher-level structured tasks.

Current models consistently struggle to capture the full variance of ground truth future datasets across most tasks, indicating a challenge in modeling the complete diversity and uncertainty of real-world dynamics.
solution: A two-stage generalist forecasting framework is proposed, where lightweight task-specific readout heads are first trained on frozen video model representations for perception tasks.

A conditional denoising latent diffusion model is then trained to forecast future latent trajectories directly in the frozen representation space of the video model, leveraging the pre-trained readout heads to decode task-specific predictions.

The framework is evaluated across diverse pre-trained vision models (image-only, masked video, video synthesis) and multiple levels of abstraction (pixels, depth, point tracks, bounding boxes) using novel distributional metrics like Fréchet Distance and variance.
paper_summary.summary: Google DeepMind introduces a two-stage generalist framework for video forecasting that leverages frozen pre-trained vision backbones and latent diffusion models to predict future events across multiple levels of abstraction. The work demonstrates a strong correlation between a vision model's perceptual ability and its generalist forecasting performance, with temporally pre-trained models exhibiting superior predictive capabilities compared to image-only or language-supervised models.

title: Generalist Forecasting with Frozen Video Models via Latent Diffusion

abstract: Forecasting the evolution of 3D scenes and generating unseen scenarios via occupancy-based world models offers substantial potential for addressing corner cases in autonomous driving systems. While tokenization has revolutionized image and video generation, efficiently tokenizing complex 3D scenes remains a critical challenge for 3D world models. To address this, we propose $I^{2}$-World, an efficient framework for 4D occupancy forecasting. Our method decouples scene tokenization into intra-scene and inter-scene tokenizers. The intra-scene tokenizer employs a multi-scale residual quantization strategy to hierarchically compress 3D scenes while preserving spatial details. The inter-scene tokenizer residually aggregates temporal dependencies across timesteps. This dual design preserves the compactness of 3D tokenizers while retaining the dynamic expressiveness of 4D tokenizers. Unlike decoder-only GPT-style autoregressive models, $I^{2}$-World adopts an encoder-decoder architecture. The encoder aggregates spatial context from the current scene and predicts a transformation matrix to enable high-level control over scene generation. The decoder, conditioned on this matrix and historical tokens, ensures temporal consistency during generation. Experiments demonstrate that $I^{2}$-World achieves state-of-the-art performance, outperforming existing methods by 25.1\% in mIoU and 36.9\% in IoU for 4D occupancy forecasting while exhibiting exceptional computational efficiency: it requires merely 2.9 GB of training memory and achieves real-time inference at 37.0 FPS. Our code is available on this https URL.

organization_info.name: National Key Laboratory of Human-Machine Hybrid Augmented Intelligence

keyInsights: Decoupling spatiotemporal tokenization into intra-scene (multi-scale residual quantization) and inter-scene (aligned historical features) effectively balances the need for detailed spatial representation with efficient temporal dynamic modeling.

Explicitly guiding autoregressive forecasting with a predicted transformation matrix (encoding spatiotemporal shifts) enhances prediction fidelity and offers intuitive, precise control over future scene evolution.

A lightweight temporal fusion mechanism within the I²-Former, combined with spatial self-attention, maintains temporal consistency and spatial coherence efficiently, avoiding complex, high-overhead recurrent or attention modules.
originalProblem: Autonomous driving systems require robust 4D (3D + time) scene forecasting for safe and informed decision-making.

Existing 3D scene tokenizers are efficient but lack temporal dynamics, while 4D tokenizers capture dynamics but incur prohibitive computational costs, making them unsuitable for real-time applications.
A critical trade-off exists between tokenization efficiency and dynamic expressiveness, hindering the practical deployment of high-fidelity 4D world models.
results: Achieved state-of-the-art performance on the Occ3D-nus validation dataset, with I²-World-O improving mIoU by 25.1% (39.73% vs. 31.75%) and IoU by 36.9% (49.80% vs. 36.36%) compared to prior methods.
Demonstrated exceptional computational efficiency, requiring only 2.9 GB of training memory and achieving real-time inference at 37.04 FPS on an RTX 4090 GPU.
Exhibited strong zero-shot generalization capabilities on the Occ3D-Waymo dataset, outperforming a copy-paste baseline significantly (e.g., 43.73% mIoU for 10Hz vs. 28.34% for baseline).
solution: The I²-World framework features an I²-Scene Tokenizer that performs decoupled spatiotemporal tokenization, separating spatial details (intra-scene) from temporal dynamics (inter-scene).
It employs an I²-Former, an encoder-decoder autoregressive model, that leverages a predicted transformation matrix to guide the forecasting of future scene tokens, conditioned on ego-motion.
The framework enables controllable scene generation through both high-level commands (e.g., 'turn left/right') and fine-grained manipulation of the ego-motion transformation matrix.
paper_summary.summary: I²-World, a framework from Xi’an Jiaotong University, introduces decoupled intra-inter tokenization and transformation matrix-guided forecasting for efficient 4D occupancy prediction. The model improves mIoU by 25.1% and IoU by 36.9% on Occ3D-nus, while achieving real-time inference at 37.04 FPS with only 2.9 GB training memory.

title: $I^{2}$-World: Intra-Inter Tokenization for Efficient Dynamic 4D Scene Forecasting

abstract: This work presents VTok, a unified video tokenization framework that can be used for both generation and understanding tasks. Unlike the leading vision-language systems that tokenize videos through a naive frame-sampling strategy, we propose to decouple the spatial and temporal representations of videos by retaining the spatial features of a single key frame while encoding each subsequent frame into a single residual token, achieving compact yet expressive video tokenization. Our experiments suggest that VTok effectively reduces the complexity of video representation from the product of frame count and per-frame token count to their sum, while the residual tokens sufficiently capture viewpoint and motion changes relative to the key frame. Extensive evaluations demonstrate the efficacy and efficiency of VTok: it achieves notably higher performance on a range of video understanding and text-to-video generation benchmarks compared with baselines using naive tokenization, all with shorter token sequences per video (e.g., 3.4% higher accuracy on our TV-Align benchmark and 1.9% higher VBench score). Remarkably, VTok produces more coherent motion and stronger guidance following in text-to-video generation, owing to its more consistent temporal encoding. We hope VTok can serve as a standardized video tokenization paradigm for future research in video understanding and generation.

keyInsights: Video frames exhibit strong inter-frame continuity and coherence, allowing for efficient representation where spatial information is primarily captured by a key frame and temporal dynamics by residual changes.
Decoupling spatial and temporal features enables a more compact and expressive video representation, transforming token complexity from a product of frame count and spatial tokens to their sum.

Jointly training a single multimodal large language model (MLLM) on both video understanding and generation tasks fosters the learning of richer, high-level semantic representations that mutually enhance performance in both domains.
originalProblem: Current video tokenization in multimodal models relies on naive frame sampling, leading to redundant spatial information and excessive token sequence lengths.
This redundancy results in high computational costs for Transformer models and necessitates sparse frame sampling, causing the loss of crucial micro-motions and transient temporal details.
The limitations impede fine-grained temporal reasoning in video understanding and lead to weak motion grounding, timing inconsistencies, and identity drift in text-to-video generation.
results: Achieved a substantial reduction in video token sequence length and computational complexity, representing a 5-second video with 46 tokens compared to much longer sequences from frame-sampling methods.

Demonstrated superior text-to-video alignment on the new TV-Align benchmark with 43.9% accuracy, outperforming leading open-source models and showing improved adherence to semantic instructions, especially for quantitative and dynamic elements.

Showed consistent performance gains across seven standard video understanding benchmarks (e.g., +1.8% for Qwen2.5-VL-7B and +2.4% for LLaVA-Next), and obtained a VBench total score of 85.16%, with notable improvements in video quality and dynamic degree (76.11%).
solution: VTok introduced a method to decouple spatial and temporal representations by encoding a "key frame" for spatial information and subsequent "residual motion tokens" for temporal changes.

The video token sequence is formed by concatenating key frame spatial tokens with residual motion tokens, reducing complexity from O(T 
 S) to O(T + S).
This tokenization is integrated into a unified multimodal framework, utilizing a pre-trained MLLM (LLaVA-Next with LLaMA3-8B) for joint video understanding and text-to-video generation.
paper_summary.summary: A unified video tokenizer, VTok, was developed to efficiently represent video content using decoupled spatial and temporal latents. This approach reduces token sequence length from product-based to sum-based complexity, leading to improved text-to-video alignment and quality, alongside consistent gains in video understanding benchmarks.

title: VTok: A Unified Video Tokenizer with Decoupled Spatial-Temporal Latents

abstract: World models learn to predict the temporal evolution of visual observations given a control signal, potentially enabling agents to reason about environments through forward simulation. Because of the focus on forward simulation, current world models generate predictions based on factual observations. For many emerging applications, such as comprehensive evaluations of physical AI behavior under varying conditions, the ability of world models to answer counterfactual queries, such as "what would happen if this object was removed?", is of increasing importance. We formalize counterfactual world models that additionally take interventions as explicit inputs, predicting temporal sequences under hypothetical modifications to observed scene properties. Traditional world models operate directly on entangled pixel-space representations where object properties and relationships cannot be selectively modified. This modeling choice prevents targeted interventions on specific scene properties. We introduce CWMDT, a framework to overcome those limitations, turning standard video diffusion models into effective counterfactual world models. First, CWMDT constructs digital twins of observed scenes to explicitly encode objects and their relationships, represented as structured text. Second, CWMDT applies large language models to reason over these representations and predict how a counterfactual intervention propagates through time to alter the observed scene. Third, CWMDT conditions a video diffusion model with the modified representation to generate counterfactual visual sequences. Evaluations on two benchmarks show that the CWMDT approach achieves state-of-the-art performance, suggesting that alternative representations of videos, such as the digital twins considered here, offer powerful control signals for video forward simulation-based world models.

keyInsights: Decomposing the counterfactual video generation task into perception, explicit reasoning over structured representations, and visual synthesis enables fine-grained control and physically plausible outcomes.

Integrating LLMs with digital twin representations provides a powerful mechanism for simulating the causal propagation of hypothetical interventions through time, addressing limitations of pixel-space modeling.

The framework advances causal AI by allowing generative models to move beyond correlational learning towards a more explicit, interpretable understanding of interventions and their effects in dynamic visual environments.
originalProblem: Current world models primarily predict factual outcomes, lacking the ability to reason about 'what would happen if' hypothetical modifications (interventions) were made.
Existing video diffusion models operate in an entangled pixel-space, preventing precise, targeted interventions on specific scene elements or their properties.
Evaluating physical AI systems robustly requires causal reasoning capabilities to understand the propagating effects of interventions across time.
results: CWMDT significantly outperformed existing baselines on the RVEBench and FiVE benchmarks, achieving up to 33.33% in GroundingDINO scores and 64.06% in LLM-as-a-Judge scores, demonstrating superior spatial localization and semantic alignment.

The system successfully generated diverse and physically plausible counterfactual video trajectories from single interventions, showcasing its capacity to explore the uncertainty inherent in hypothetical scenarios.

Ablation studies confirmed the critical contributions of digital twin representations, LLM-based intervention reasoning, and the use of a modified initial frame for accurate and consistent counterfactual video generation.
solution: The CWMDT framework constructs structured 'digital twin' representations (JSON objects) of initial video frames, explicitly encoding object details, attributes, and spatial properties using multiple vision foundation models.

A Large Language Model (LLM) performs counterfactual reasoning over these digital twin representations based on natural language intervention queries, predicting the temporal evolution of changes to generate modified digital twin sequences.

A fine-tuned video diffusion model synthesizes counterfactual video sequences conditioned on the modified digital twin representations and an edited initial frame that reflects the immediate intervention effects.
paper_summary.summary: A framework for counterfactual world models generates hypothetical video sequences by integrating digital twin representations, large language models for causal reasoning, and video diffusion models for synthesis. This approach significantly improves performance on counterfactual video generation and fine-grained video editing benchmarks, achieving up to triple the spatial grounding accuracy of prior methods.

title: Counterfactual World Models via Digital Twin-conditioned Video Diffusion

abstract: World foundation models aim to simulate the evolution of the real world with physically plausible behavior. Unlike prior methods that handle spatial and temporal correlations separately, we propose RAYNOVA, a geometry-agonistic multiview world model for driving scenarios that employs a dual-causal autoregressive framework. It follows both scale-wise and temporal topological orders in the autoregressive process, and leverages global attention for unified 4D spatio-temporal reasoning. Different from existing works that impose strong 3D geometric priors, RAYNOVA constructs an isotropic spatio-temporal representation across views, frames, and scales based on relative Plücker-ray positional encoding, enabling robust generalization to diverse camera setups and ego motions. We further introduce a recurrent training paradigm to alleviate distribution drift in long-horizon video generation. RAYNOVA achieves state-of-the-art multi-view video generation results on nuScenes, while offering higher throughput and strong controllability under diverse input conditions, generalizing to novel views and camera configurations without explicit 3D scene representation. Our code will be released at this https URL.

keyInsights: Unifying scale-wise and temporal reasoning within a dual-causal autoregressive framework provides a more coherent approach to spatio-temporal modeling for multi-view video generation.
An isotropic relative position embedding in camera ray space enables robust generalization to diverse camera setups and novel views without relying on explicit 3D inductive biases.
Recurrent training with error simulation effectively bridges the training-inference distribution gap, improving temporal coherence for extended video generation.
originalProblem: Existing world models often decouple spatial and temporal reasoning, limiting flexibility in adapting to novel sensor configurations or rapid camera motions.
Reliance on strong 3D geometric priors (e.g., point clouds, BEV features) restricts generalization to unconstrained open-world environments.
Autoregressive models suffer from distribution drift during long-horizon video generation due to mismatches between training and inference input distributions.
results: Achieved state-of-the-art multi-view video generation performance on nuScenes, with FID 10.5 and FVD 91, outperforming previous models.

Demonstrated high fidelity to object and map conditions; synthetic videos enabled multi-sensor perception models to achieve NDS of 72.0 (99% of Oracle) for objects and mIoU of 49.9 (79% of Oracle) for maps.
Exhibited zero-shot generalization to unseen camera configurations (e.g., Waymo) and strong novel view synthesis capabilities, showing only slight degradation for camera shifts up to 4m.
solution: Proposed RAYNOVA, a geometry-agnostic multiview world model utilizing a dual-causal autoregressive framework that processes both scale and time.
Developed an isotropic 4D position embedding based on relative Plücker rays in camera ray space for unified spatio-temporal representation.
Implemented a recurrent training paradigm with latent feature caching and random bitwise error injection to mitigate distribution shift in long-horizon video generation.
paper_summary.summary: RAYNOVA introduces a dual-causal autoregressive world foundation model that generates multi-view videos by unifying scale and temporal reasoning through an isotropic spatio-temporal representation in camera ray space. It achieved an FID of 10.5 and FVD of 91 on the nuScenes dataset, demonstrating robust generalization to novel camera configurations and high fidelity to object and map conditions.

title: RAYNOVA: Scale-Temporal Autoregressive World Modeling in Ray Space

abstract: World model based planning has significantly improved decision-making in complex environments by enabling agents to simulate future states and make informed choices. This computational burden is particularly restrictive in robotics, where resources are severely constrained. To address this limitation, we propose a Sparse Imagination for Efficient Visual World Model Planning, which enhances computational efficiency by reducing the number of tokens processed during forward prediction. Our method leverages a sparsely trained vision-based world model based on transformers with randomized grouped attention strategy, allowing the model to flexibly adjust the number of tokens processed based on the computational resource. By enabling sparse imagination during latent rollout, our approach significantly accelerates planning while maintaining high control fidelity. Experimental results demonstrate that sparse imagination preserves task performance while dramatically improving inference efficiency. This general technique for visual planning is applicable from simple test-time trajectory optimization to complex real-world tasks with the latest VLAs, enabling the deployment of world models in real-time scenarios.
title: Sparse Imagination for Efficient Visual World Model Planning

abstract:
Predicting future video frames is essential for decision-making systems, yet
RGB frames alone often lack the information needed to fully capture the
underlying complexities of the real world. To address this limitation, we
propose a multi-modal framework for Synchronous Video Prediction (SyncVP) that
incorporates complementary data modalities, enhancing the richness and accuracy
of future predictions. SyncVP builds on pre-trained modality-specific diffusion
models and introduces an efficient spatio-temporal cross-attention module to
enable effective information sharing across modalities. We evaluate SyncVP on
standard benchmark datasets, such as Cityscapes and BAIR, using depth as an
additional modality. We furthermore demonstrate its generalization to other
modalities on SYNTHIA with semantic information and ERA5-Land with climate
data. Notably, SyncVP achieves state-of-the-art performance, even in scenarios
where only one modality is present, demonstrating its robustness and potential
for a wide range of applications.

organization_info.name: Lamarr Institute for Machine Learning and Artificial Intelligence

keyInsights: Explicitly sharing the noise vector across modalities during the forward diffusion process is crucial for enforcing synchronous predictions, leading to faster loss convergence and improved conditional generation.
The proposed Spatio-Temporal Cross-Attention (STCA) module efficiently aligns high-level semantic features between modalities with reduced computational complexity compared to naive cross-attention.
Training with Cross-Modality Guidance (CMG) enables the model to robustly predict future frames for all modalities, even when only a subset of input modalities is provided.
originalProblem: Traditional single-modality video prediction, typically RGB-only, lacks comprehensive contextual cues for fully capturing complex real-world dynamics.
Existing multi-modal generative models primarily condition one modality's generation with another, rather than performing truly symmetric and simultaneous prediction of multiple future modalities.
Efficiently exchanging information between diverse data streams for video prediction without incurring excessive computational cost remains a challenge.
results: SyncVP achieved new state-of-the-art performance on Cityscapes and BAIR, improving Frechet Video Distance (FVD) by over 21% and 27% respectively.
The framework demonstrated robustness to partial conditioning, successfully generating highly aligned and consistent frames for missing modalities (e.g., depth from only RGB input).

Generalization across diverse datasets and modality types was confirmed, including RGB-D, RGB-semantic segmentation (SYNTHIA), and climate data prediction (ERA5-Land), consistently outperforming single-modality baselines.
solution: SyncVP employs a dual-branch latent diffusion model, where each branch processes a compact 2D latent representation of a specific modality using a customized UNet architecture.

A Spatio-Temporal Cross-Attention (STCA) module, strategically placed between the deepest layers of the UNet branches, facilitates efficient cross-modality information exchange using a single shared attention map.

A two-stage training process involves pre-training individual modality diffusion models, followed by joint fine-tuning with a shared noise vector across modalities and Cross-Modality Guidance (CMG) to handle partial conditioning.
paper_summary.summary: SyncVP introduces a latent diffusion framework for synchronously predicting multiple future video modalities, such as RGB and depth, from multi-modal input sequences. The approach achieves new state-of-the-art performance on benchmarks like Cityscapes and BAIR while demonstrating robustness to partial input conditioning and effective generalization across diverse data types, including climate data.

title: SyncVP: Joint Diffusion for Synchronous Multi-Modal Video Prediction

abstract:
Our work explores the task of generating future sensor observations
conditioned on the past. We are motivated by `predictive coding' concepts from
neuroscience as well as robotic applications such as self-driving vehicles.
Predictive video modeling is challenging because the future may be multi-modal
and learning at scale remains computationally expensive for video processing.
To address both challenges, our key insight is to leverage the large-scale
pretraining of image diffusion models which can handle multi-modality. We
repurpose image models for video prediction by conditioning on new frame
timestamps. Such models can be trained with videos of both static and dynamic
scenes. To allow them to be trained with modestly-sized datasets, we introduce
invariances by factoring out illumination and texture by forcing the model to
predict (pseudo) depth, readily obtained for in-the-wild videos via
off-the-shelf monocular depth networks. In fact, we show that simply modifying
networks to predict grayscale pixels already improves the accuracy of video
prediction. Given the extra controllability with timestamp conditioning, we
propose sampling schedules that work better than the traditional autoregressive
and hierarchical sampling strategies. Motivated by probabilistic metrics from
the object forecasting literature, we create a benchmark for video prediction
on a diverse set of videos spanning indoor and outdoor scenes and a large
vocabulary of objects. Our experiments illustrate the effectiveness of learning
to condition on timestamps, and show the importance of predicting the future
with invariant modalities.

keyInsights: Large-scale 2D image diffusion models can be efficiently repurposed for complex video prediction tasks, significantly lowering the computational barrier for developing powerful video generative models.

Explicit timestamp conditioning transforms a static image generation model into a flexible temporal prediction engine, enabling versatile applications like variable framerate video generation, frame interpolation, and backcasting.
Predicting invariant modalities such as pseudo-depth or luminance simplifies the learning task, offering more robust performance and directly actionable cues for embodied AI systems.
Training with a 'random timestamp prediction' objective, similar to masked autoencoders, enhances the model's robustness and temporal understanding for forecasting tasks.
originalProblem: Video prediction is challenging due to the multi-modal nature of future events and the high dimensionality of video data.
Training powerful video models from scratch requires extensive datasets and significant computational resources, which can be prohibitive.
Embodied AI systems, such as autonomous vehicles, need accurate forecasts of future environmental states for robust motion planning and decision-making.
results: The method achieved superior performance on the TAO dataset for +1s single-frame depth forecasting, with a Top-1 L1 error of 8.40, outperforming state-of-the-art baselines like MCVD (10.54) and FDM (9.99).

For long-horizon multi-frame forecasting (+10s sequences), the proposed 'Mixed sampling' strategy yielded the best performance with a Top-1 ATE of 12.16, surpassing autoregressive (15.20) and hierarchical (15.15) sampling.
Predicting pseudo-depth directly achieved a Top-5 L1 error of 6.59, which was significantly better than inferring depth from luminance (17.61) or RGB (19.33) predictions.
The model was fine-tuned efficiently (7 hours on 8 NVIDIA RTX A6000s) from pre-trained Stable Diffusion weights, and even training from scratch on a modest dataset outperformed all other baselines.
solution: Adapt pre-trained 2D image diffusion models by fine-tuning them for timestamp-conditioned video forecasting, leveraging their inherent generative capabilities.
Introduce explicit timestamp conditioning using positionally encoded, continuous-valued timestamps to enable generation of frames at arbitrary future or past times.
Focus on predicting invariant modalities like pseudo-depth or luminance rather than raw RGB pixels, simplifying the learning problem and providing directly actionable geometric information.
Employ novel sampling schedules, including a 'Mixed sampling' strategy, to combine direct and autoregressive signals for improved accuracy and temporal coherence in long-horizon predictions.
paper_summary.summary: Carnegie Mellon University researchers present an efficient framework for long-horizon video prediction by repurposing pre-trained 2D image diffusion models, using explicit timestamp conditioning and invariant modalities like pseudo-depth. This approach delivers superior performance in forecasting and offers flexible temporal generation crucial for embodied AI applications.

title: Predicting Long-horizon Futures by Conditioning on Geometry and Time

abstract: Recent advances in Video Large Language Models (VLLMs) have achieved remarkable video understanding capabilities, yet face critical efficiency bottlenecks due to quadratic computational growth with lengthy visual token sequences of long videos.
